dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 27 for unconscious patient at Franklin and Broad.
Hi, I'm Dana, a paramedic with the rescue squad. Can you tell me what happened?
He was walking and just collapsed. We couldn't wake him up.
Can you open your eyes for me, sir?
He's not opening his eyes.
Sir, what is your name?
He doesn't move or respond at all to any stimuli at this time.
Airway is patent. I'll assess breathing now to determine need for airway intervention.
He's breathing 12 times per minute, shallow, and seems irregular but not apneic.
Radial pulse is strong and regular, heart rate is 105 beats per minute, and blood pressure is 168/91 mmHg.
SpO2 is 87%, which is low-consider supplemental oxygen after airway assessment.
Can someone tell me what he was doing just before he fell down? Any unusual behaviors or symptoms prior to losing consciousness?.
He was fine one minute, then apparently lost balance and fell suddenly. He didn't scream or grab his chest or anything-just collapsed without warning.
There is blood coming from his nose-probably broken. No immediately visible signs of trauma or injuries to the head or limbs right now.
Is there anyone here who knows the patient's age or name?
Yes, he's a 40-year-old man with no known identification here on his person or with the bystanders present at this time.
Skin is warm, dry, and pink. I don't see any signs of pallor or flushing, which is good for a non-shock state.

Pupils are pinpoint and initially not fully reactive. We'll reassess after any medications are administered and vitals are stabilized, given the potential for opioid exposure shown in the past behavior and the response after Narcan administration.
Sir, do you feel any pain or discomfort in any part of your body at the moment?
Heart rate remains at 105 beats per minute with no change in the last five minutes and the trend seems stable at this time following initial monitoring.
Respiration rate is now 12 per minute, shallow-still not showing full respiratory support on his own efforts without assistance, but not in severe distress observed here.
Blood pressure is currently 168/91 mmHg-elevated, but not immediately life-threatening for now.
Oxygen saturation at 87%-this remains low, so we will encourage the use of supplemental oxygen once IV access is established.
Glucose level is 263 mg/dL-this suggests overt hyperglycemia.
EKG currently shows sinus tachycardia-nothing suspicious for ST elevation or other signs of acute coronary issues at this point.
We will give 1 mg of Narcan via IV to reverse any likely opioid overdose effects given the current state of unconsciousness seen in initial assessment.
16-gauge IV successfully started in the left upper arm with a 10 mL saline flush to confirm patency of the system.
4-lead ECG monitoring initiated to detect any additional cardiac rhythm signs after medication administration.
Respiration has improved to 16 per minute, regular rate and seems to show improvement in tidal volume without signs of struggle or effort.
Pulse increased to 111 beats per minute after Narcan likely secondary to sympathetic activation-still in range but elevated.
SpO2 is now at 95%, significantly improved since initial measurement of 87%-indicative of response either to Narcan alone or in conjunction with IV push administration.
Glucose test was attempted but remained at 0 mg/dL-too slow or delayed to detect valid level.
Blood pressure is 124/78 mmHg-this suggests some hemodynamic stabilization secondary to elevated heart rate after Narcan administration.
Both pupils now appear 2 mm and reactive to light-indicative of some response and improvement post-Narcan administration.
He doesn't report pain or discomfort at this time.
Respiration is at 16 per minute with regular depth and sufficient tidal volume-continuing to monitor for possible deterioration but no signs of respiratory distress seen in the last ten minutes.

No signs of chest discomfort, shortness of breath, or dizziness observed at this time-seems fully responsive from a mental standpoint given a score of 15 on GCS following initial score of 3 upon unconsciousness.
Do you have any known chronic medical conditions or pre-existing issues like diabetes, asthma, or hypertension that we need to consider here?
Are you known to have any allergic reactions to medications, foods, or may have experienced past allergy-like symptoms during medical visits or mishap events?
I am not aware of any drug, food, or latte allergies.
Are you currently taking any medicines or supplements that could be affecting your condition now?
I don't take any medications currently.
What was the last time you had something to eat or drink?
I had something about four hours ago.
What exactly were you doing before you collapsed? Can you tell me if you were exercising, resting, alongside any other disturbance to your routine immediately before this started?
I just finished a shower, was walking and feeling well. I was relaxed, didn't feel any numbness or pressure before that moment when I fell down.
12-lead ECG performed showing sinus tachycardia with some minor T-wave changes but no significant ST segment abnormalities noted.
Some dried blood still visible in nasopharynx making oxygen cannula placement for ETCO2 screening attempts difficult.
Respiratory rate is 16 breaths per minute with normal rate and depth-no signs of struggle or labored breathing at this time.
ETCO2 measurement could not be obtained due to nasal obstruction caused by dried blood.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 15, up from an initial 3-improving significantly after Narcan administration at pre-transit stage.
No new symptoms reported-still no dizziness or signs of worsening.
No signs of nausea or vomiting observed, and vitals including SpO2 and BP remain stable.
EKG still showing sinus tachycardia, consistent with the initial findings.
No signs of shortness of breath or headache observed at this time-confirming current stability for safe transport.
Radial pulse remains palpable and circulating effectively-no signs of hemodynamic instability or circulatory issues.
Has there been any recent use of any opioid drugs, heroin or recreational drugs including street opiates or any cannabis type?
No heroin use, but I do drink alcohol and smoked some marijuana recently.
We have administered Narcan via IV push and followed up with a 10 mL saline flush.
Blood pressure is now 141/81 mmHg, indicating continued hemodynamic stability and no concerning drop or rise in blood pressure at this stage.
Pulse is now down to 94 beats per minute, suggesting some relaxation in heart rate post-intervention without any concerning signs of instability or drop below baseline as seen on final readings.
Vitals obtained and monitored as documented, including the latest BP and pulse readings to ensure ongoing assessment accuracy during transport.
Handing over care with full report after transfer to bed 15 for continuity of care at VCU ED.
All documentation transferred and explained to hospital staff. Case summary, interventions performed, and vital signs at the time of transport are clearly presented.
Narcan was administered via IV push with a 10 mL saline flush-spO2 improved from 87% to 95% right after administration.
Patient denied any swallowing difficulties or history of diabetic episodes.
Full decontamination performed en route to the hospital to address blood contamination.
Patient transferred to hospital bed 15 directly from stretcher as planned-seamless handoff with hospital care initiated immediately upon arrival.
Final note includes stable patient and coordination passed to ED for handover-no further EMS care needed at this time.
We will transport you to VCU Medical Center for a more detailed evaluation and monitoring.
